---
title: 'DRAFT - Nerdy Biblical Theology Majors Group Guidelines'
date: '2025-05-11T07:11:00.0040000+00:00'
draft: false
blogger_blog: 'NoHiding.Faith'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '2959722282588679390'
blogger_filename: '/2025/05/draft-nerdy-biblical-theology-majors.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/NoHiding.Faith/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'NoHiding Faith Studies'
---

I wrote this for a group I was participating in, before I got too busy. Saving it here as a good reference.&nbsp;<div><br /></div><div><div style="border-top: solid #4EA72E 1.0pt; border: none; mso-border-top-themecolor: accent6; mso-element: para-border-div; padding: 1.0pt 0in 0in 0in;">

<p align="left" class="MsoTitle">***DRAFT*** Nerdy Biblical
Theology Majors<o:p></o:p></p>

</div>

<p align="left" class="MsoQuote"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoQuote">“A text without a context
is just a pretext for whatever you want it to mean; therefore always study the
Bible in light of its original historical, archaeological, literary,
theological, ethical contexts” – Ben Witherington III. “Reading and
Understanding the Bible: An Interview with Ben Witherington III.” Interview by
Jonathan Petersen. Blog Post of Interview, 11 December 2014. Bible Gateway Blog
[<a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/blog/2014/12/reading-understanding-the-bible-an-interview-with-ben-witherington-iii/">LINK</a>].<o:p></o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<h1>Nerdy Biblical Theology Majors Group Guidelines<o:p></o:p></h1>

<h2>Purpose:<o:p></o:p></h2>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8pt;"><br />
This group exists to explore Biblical Theology, focusing on understanding
biblical texts within their original historical and literary contexts.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8pt;">We explore the language, culture, and intertextuality of the
biblical authors within their original historical contexts and literary
ecosystems.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8pt;">We aim to foster thoughtful, text-based discussion, welcoming
contributions from academics, students, and laypersons alike.<o:p></o:p></p>

<h2>Core Guidelines:<o:p></o:p></h2>

<h3 style="margin-bottom: 0in; mso-list: l11 level1 lfo1;"><!--[if !supportLists]-->1.<span style="font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-emoji: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; font-weight: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span>Stay <span class="Heading3Char">Textual</span>:<o:p></o:p></h3>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal" style="margin-left: 0.5in;">In
general, posts should be grounded in a specific biblical text or series of
inter-related textual themes. We’re here to discuss the Bible in its original
context, not abstract theology. When contributing, please make it clear what verse,
pericope, book, or textual theme you’re working with. Discussions about
doctrines, creeds, or theological systems are <i>generally discouraged</i>, but
may sometimes be appropriate when anchored to the biblical text itself.<o:p></o:p></p>

<ol start="1" style="margin-top: 0in;" type="1">
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level2 lfo1; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><i>Acceptable: “How
      does Paul’s use of ‘righteousness’ in Romans reflect Second Temple Jewish
      thought?”</i><o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level2 lfo1; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><i>Not acceptable:
      “Is (insert generic theology term) correct?”</i><o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level1 lfo1; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><span class="Heading3Char">Academic Sources Encouraged, but Popular Sources
     Allowed:</span><br />
     We aim for an academic tone, but we recognize that laymen and students
     bring value too. When possible, cite academic sources such as
     peer-reviewed journal articles, books from academic publishers (Baker,
     IVP, Zondervan, etc.), or academic lexicons like <b>BDB</b> or <b>HALOT</b>
     or <b>BDAG</b>. Popular sources are welcome, provided they contribute <i>constructively</i>
     to the conversation and engage meaningfully with the biblical text.<o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level2 lfo1; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><i>Acceptable: A
      YouTube video from a scholar or student who cites their sources.</i><o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level2 lfo1; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><i>Not acceptable: YouTube
      where someone is not using an academic or scholarly tone or approach, or
      outdated materials like Strong’s Concordance.</i><o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level1 lfo1; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><span class="Heading3Char">Engage Respectfully and Openly</span><b>:</b><br />
     This is a place for <b>discussion, not debate</b>. Ask genuine questions
     of the group, be open to alternate readings, and engage in conversation
     with curiosity, not defensiveness. Posts should encourage dialogue rather
     than monologues, inviting others to share their perspectives.<o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level2 lfo1; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><i>Example: “I’ve
      been thinking about this reading of Genesis—what do you think?”</i><o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level2 lfo1; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><i>Example: “Have you
      considered…?”</i><o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level1 lfo1; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><span class="Heading3Char">Structure Your Contributions for Readability</span><b>:</b><br />
     Posts should be easy to read, especially on mobile. Use paragraphs,
     punctuation, and clear structure. <o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level2 lfo1; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;">Conciseness is
      key—keep it digestible while making your point. If your post is long,
      consider breaking it up or summarizing key points at the start. <o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level2 lfo1; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;">Consider providing a
      simple summary of the issue and linking it to an academic source for the
      broader discussion.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level2 lfo1; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;">Ask smaller
      questions, focus on one key point of discussion, one key word or phrase
      or theme.<o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level1 lfo1; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><span class="Heading3Char">Avoid Systematic Theology, Creeds, and Traditions</span><b>:</b><br />
     While systematic theology, doctrines, and creeds have their place, this
     group is dedicated to <b>biblical theology</b>—understanding the text as
     it was written in its own time. Posts focused on systems or traditions
     without textual grounding will be redirected or removed.<o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level2 lfo1; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><span class="Heading3Char">What did the original author intend to communicate to
      his/her original ancient audience?</span><o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level1 lfo1; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><span class="Heading3Char">No Politics or Denominational or Fringe Debates</span><b>:</b><br />
     To maintain focus, political discussions and denominational disputes are
     not allowed. Keep the focus on the biblical text and avoid getting bogged
     down in eschatological debates, Calvinism vs. Arminianism debates, or
     similar well-worn theological conflicts. The late Dr. Michael Heiser used
     to “if it’s weird, it’s important”. So, we’re not against odd takes, but
     they should be rooted in the text and within solid academic evaluation of
     the text. Let’s avoid ranting YouTubers.<o:p></o:p></li>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level1 lfo1; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><span class="Heading3Char">Post with Purpose</span><b>:</b><br />
     Make sure your post has a clear goal aimed at inviting a conversation—whether
     it's asking a question, presenting a thought, or inviting feedback. Posts
     should not simply state opinions without backing them up with evidence or
     questions.<o:p></o:p></li>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8.0pt; mso-list: l11 level1 lfo1; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><b>No promotions or
     spam<o:p></o:p></b></li>
</ol>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin: 0in 0in 8pt 0.5in;">Give
more than you take in this group. Self-promotion, spam and irrelevant links
aren't allowed. No solicitations for money or asking someone to pay for
something.<o:p></o:p></p>

<div align="left" class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8pt;">

<hr align="left" size="2" width="100%" />

</div>

<h2>Need Clarification?<o:p></o:p></h2>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8pt;"><br />
If you’re unsure about what qualifies as biblical theology or whether your post
fits the group’s focus, please review our <span class="MsoHyperlink">Definitions Post</span> or ask an admin for guidance.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<h1>Alt Info<o:p></o:p></h1>

<div style="border-bottom: solid windowtext 1.0pt; border-left: none; border-right: none; border-top: solid windowtext 1.0pt; mso-border-bottom-alt: solid windowtext .75pt; mso-border-top-alt: solid windowtext .75pt; mso-element: para-border-div; padding: 1.0pt 0in 1.0pt 0in;">

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal" style="border: none; padding: 0in;"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

</div>

<p align="left" class="MsoListParagraphCxSpFirst" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8pt; text-indent: -0.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-emoji: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><span class="Heading3Char">The
Hebrew Bible</span> (Tanakh, aka Old Testament) is understood within its
Ancient Near East (ANE) contexts. <o:p></o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle" style="line-height: 115%; margin-bottom: 8pt; text-indent: -0.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; mso-bidi-font-family: Symbol; mso-fareast-font-family: Symbol;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-emoji: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><span class="Heading3Char">The
Greek Bible</span> (New Covenant, B’rit Chadashah, aka New Testament) is
understood within its Second Temple Judaism and Greco-Roman world, contexts.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoListParagraphCxSpLast" style="text-indent: -0.25in;"><!--[if !supportLists]--><span style="font-family: Symbol; font-size: 12pt; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-caps: small-caps; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-emoji: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; letter-spacing: 0.25pt; line-height: 115%;">·<span style="font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; font-feature-settings: normal; font-kerning: auto; font-optical-sizing: auto; font-size-adjust: none; font-size: 7pt; font-stretch: normal; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-caps: normal; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-emoji: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; font-variation-settings: normal; line-height: normal;">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
</span></span><!--[endif]--><span dir="LTR"></span><span class="Heading3Char">Additional
Texts </span>(Apocrypha, Pseudepigrapha, Dead Sea Scrolls/Qumran writings, Ugaritic
&amp; Akkadian writings, Greco-Roman philosophers, and other comparative
literature) are included in discussions to provide greater insight into the
cultural and religious environment of the biblical authors. These texts help us
understand how the biblical texts interacted with other literature of the time
and what influence they may have had on early Jewish and Christian thought.
They should be used to understand a biblical text but not become the sole focus
of the post.<span style="font-size: 12pt; font-variant-alternates: normal; font-variant-caps: small-caps; font-variant-east-asian: normal; font-variant-emoji: normal; font-variant-numeric: normal; font-variant-position: normal; letter-spacing: 0.25pt; line-height: 115%;"><o:p></o:p></span></p>

<div style="border-bottom: solid windowtext 1.0pt; border-left: none; border-right: none; border-top: solid windowtext 1.0pt; mso-border-bottom-alt: solid windowtext .75pt; mso-border-top-alt: solid windowtext .75pt; mso-element: para-border-div; padding: 1.0pt 0in 1.0pt 0in;">

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal" style="border: none; padding: 0in;"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

</div>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal">***DRAFT***<o:p></o:p></p>

<h1>NERDY BIBLICAL THEOLOGY MAJORS - DEFINITIONS POST<o:p></o:p></h1>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal">To help clarify key terms
and ensure discussions stay focused, we’ve provided definitions for some
foundational concepts and terms frequently used in this group. Please reference
these definitions when engaging in discussions, and feel free to ask questions
if you're unsure whether your post fits within these boundaries.<o:p></o:p></p>

<div align="left" class="MsoNormal">

<hr align="left" size="2" width="100%" />

</div>

<h2>Biblical Theology<o:p></o:p></h2>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><b>Definition:</b>
Biblical Theology is the study of the Bible with a focus on understanding how
the biblical authors communicated theological concepts within their own
historical, cultural, and literary contexts. It emphasizes the progressive
development of ideas within the Bible itself, without imposing later systems or
doctrines.<o:p></o:p></p>

<ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="disc">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l4 level1 lfo3; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><i>Example:</i> Exploring how Paul’s letters develop ideas
     about resurrection within the Second Temple Jewish context.<o:p></o:p></li>
</ul>

<h2>Systematic Theology<o:p></o:p></h2>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><b>Definition:</b>
Systematic Theology organizes theological ideas into a structured framework,
often grouping concepts (e.g., sin, salvation, God) into categories that span
the whole Bible. While systematic theology can be helpful, this group focuses
on <i>biblical theology</i>, so discussions of systems must remain grounded in
specific biblical texts and contexts.<o:p></o:p></p>

<ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="disc">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l12 level1 lfo4; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><i>Example:</i> Instead of discussing
     "salvation" broadly, we explore how specific texts like Romans
     or the Gospel of John address salvation within their historical settings.<o:p></o:p></li>
</ul>

<h2>Ancient Near East (ANE)<o:p></o:p></h2>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><b>Definition:</b> The <b>ANE</b>
refers to the region and cultures surrounding ancient Israel, including Egypt,
Mesopotamia, Assyria, Babylon, and Canaan. Understanding the cultural,
religious, and political contexts of these civilizations helps illuminate the
Hebrew Bible.<o:p></o:p></p>

<ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="disc">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l13 level1 lfo5; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><i>Example:</i> The covenant structure of Deuteronomy
     reflects similar treaties from the ANE.<o:p></o:p></li>
</ul>

<h2>Second Temple Judaism<o:p></o:p></h2>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><b>Definition:</b> This
period (roughly 516 BCE to 70 CE) refers to the time between the rebuilding of
the Jewish temple after the Babylonian exile and its destruction by the Romans.
Many of the writings in the New Testament were shaped by the religious,
political, and cultural dynamics of this era.<o:p></o:p></p>

<ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="disc">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l3 level1 lfo6; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><i>Example:</i> Understanding Pharisees, Sadducees, and
     Messianic expectations in this period helps contextualize Jesus'
     interactions with these groups in the Gospels.<o:p></o:p></li>
</ul>

<h2>Greco-Roman World<o:p></o:p></h2>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><b>Definition:</b> This
refers to the cultural, political, and intellectual environment dominated by
Greek and Roman influence during the New Testament era. Knowing this context
helps us understand how early Christian writers like Paul engaged with
Greco-Roman society.<o:p></o:p></p>

<ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="disc">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l0 level1 lfo7; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><i>Example:</i> Paul's use of household codes in Ephesians
     reflects common Greco-Roman structures, but he subverts them with
     Christian ethics.<o:p></o:p></li>
</ul>

<h2>Apocrypha and Pseudepigrapha<o:p></o:p></h2>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><b>Definition:</b> These
are collections of Jewish writings from the intertestamental period (between
the Old and New Testaments). While not considered canon by most Protestant
traditions, these texts were widely read by Jews and early Christians and
provide important context for biblical writings.<o:p></o:p></p>

<ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="disc">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l9 level1 lfo8; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><i>Example:</i> The Book of Tobit or 1 Enoch offers insight
     into Jewish thought during the Second Temple period.<o:p></o:p></li>
</ul>

<h2><span lang="ES-MX">Lexicon (BDB, HALOT, BDAG,
etc.)<o:p></o:p></span></h2>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><b>Definition:</b> A <b>lexicon</b>
is a scholarly dictionary of ancient languages, offering definitions and
explanations of how words were used in their original contexts.<o:p></o:p></p>

<ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="disc">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l5 level1 lfo9; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><b>BDB</b> (Brown-Driver-Briggs) and <b>HALOT</b> (Hebrew and
     Aramaic Lexicon of the Old Testament) are two of the most widely respected
     lexicons for biblical Hebrew studies.<o:p></o:p></li>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l5 level1 lfo9; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><b>BDAG (Bauer-Danker-Arndt-Gingrich)</b> <i>A Greek-English
     Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature</i><o:p></o:p></li>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l5 level1 lfo9; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><i>Example:</i> Citing HALOT for the meaning of a Hebrew word
     in Isaiah adds depth to understanding the text's original meaning.<o:p></o:p></li>
</ul>

<h2>Intertextuality<o:p></o:p></h2>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><b>Definition:</b>
Intertextuality refers to the way biblical texts reference, echo, or engage
with other texts, both within the Bible and in external literature from
surrounding cultures. It helps us see connections and influences across
different works.<o:p></o:p></p>

<ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="disc">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l6 level1 lfo10; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><i>Example:</i> The use of Exodus themes in Isaiah or
     the allusions to the Psalms in the Gospels.<o:p></o:p></li>
</ul>

<div align="left" class="MsoNormal">

<hr align="left" size="2" width="100%" />

</div>

<span style="font-family: &quot;Aptos&quot;,sans-serif; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 115%; mso-ansi-language: EN-US; mso-ascii-theme-font: minor-latin; mso-bidi-font-family: Arial; mso-bidi-language: HE; mso-bidi-theme-font: minor-bidi; mso-fareast-font-family: &quot;Times New Roman&quot;; mso-fareast-language: EN-US; mso-fareast-theme-font: minor-fareast; mso-hansi-theme-font: minor-latin;">

<ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="disc">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l4 level1 lfo3; text-align: left;"><b>Post
     Guidelines Recap<o:p></o:p></b></li>
</ul>

</span>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal">If your post involves
systematic theology, creeds, or later traditions, remember that we aim to focus
on <b>biblical theology</b>, so always anchor your discussion to specific texts
and their original contexts. We welcome academic sources and encourage
respectful dialogue.<o:p></o:p></p>

<div align="left" class="MsoNormal">

<hr align="left" size="2" width="100%" />

</div>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><b>Questions or
Suggestions?</b><br />
If you're uncertain about any of these definitions or terms, feel free to reach
out to an admin or ask for clarification in the comments.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal">***DRAFT***<o:p></o:p></p>

<h1>Lexicons for Language Studies<o:p></o:p></h1>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal">Here are some of the most
widely accepted and respected academic lexicons for biblical Hebrew and Greek,
including both older foundational works and more modern resources:<o:p></o:p></p>

<h2>For Biblical Hebrew (and Aramaic):<o:p></o:p></h2>

<ol start="1" style="margin-top: 0in;" type="1">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level1 lfo11; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><b>Brown-Driver-Briggs (BDB)</b> –<o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><i>Full title:</i> <i>A Hebrew and English Lexicon
      of the Old Testament</i><o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;">Published in 1906, this lexicon remains a
      widely-used standard for biblical Hebrew and Aramaic, despite its age. It
      is respected for its comprehensive definitions and historical insights.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Pros:</b> Easily accessible, widely referenced,
      detailed word studies.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Cons:</b> Some outdated interpretations; later
      scholarship has refined certain definitions.<o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level1 lfo11; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><b>HALOT (Hebrew and Aramaic Lexicon of the Old
     Testament)</b> –<o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><i>Full title:</i> <i>The Hebrew and Aramaic
      Lexicon of the Old Testament</i> (edited by Ludwig Koehler and Walter
      Baumgartner)<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;">This multi-volume lexicon is a more recent and
      comprehensive resource. It reflects modern linguistic scholarship and
      incorporates insights from Ugaritic and other ANE languages.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Pros:</b> Up-to-date, covers both Hebrew and
      Aramaic, includes comparative Semitic language insights.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Cons:</b> Expensive, more technical.<o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level1 lfo11; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><b>Gesenius' Hebrew-Chaldee Lexicon</b> –<o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;">Published in the 19th century by Wilhelm Gesenius,
      this lexicon was one of the foundational works in biblical Hebrew
      lexicography and remains valuable for historical study.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Pros:</b> Widely accessible, foundational in
      lexicon studies.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l8 level2 lfo11; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Cons:</b> Somewhat outdated; modern scholarship
      has refined many entries.<o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
</ol>

<h1>For Biblical Greek:<o:p></o:p></h1>

<ol start="1" style="margin-top: 0in;" type="1">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level1 lfo12; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><b>BDAG (Bauer-Danker-Arndt-Gingrich)</b> –<o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><i>Full title:</i> <a name="_Hlk180790124"><i>A
      Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian
      Literature</i></a><o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;">This is the gold standard for New Testament Greek.
      BDAG is comprehensive, modern, and indispensable for scholarly work in
      biblical Greek.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Pros:</b> Thorough, reflects modern scholarship,
      includes references to early Christian literature beyond the New
      Testament.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Cons:</b> Can be dense for beginners, expensive.<o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level1 lfo12; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><b>LSJ (Liddell-Scott-Jones)</b> –<o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><i>Full title:</i> <i>A Greek-English Lexicon</i><o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;">LSJ is the most comprehensive lexicon for classical
      Greek, but it is also highly relevant for biblical and Septuagint
      studies. It is essential for understanding the broader use of Greek in
      the ancient world.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Pros:</b> Extensive coverage of Greek, including
      non-biblical sources; freely accessible online in some versions.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Cons:</b> Not specific to biblical Greek, so it
      requires careful discernment when applying to the New Testament.<o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level1 lfo12; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><b>Thayer’s Greek-English Lexicon</b> –<o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;">An older lexicon, published in the late 19th
      century. While dated, it was a widely used resource for biblical Greek
      and still finds use today in some circles.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Pros:</b> Accessible and clear for students.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l1 level2 lfo12; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Cons:</b> Many entries are outdated compared to
      BDAG; better used alongside more modern resources.<o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
</ol>

<h1>For Both Greek and Hebrew (Septuagint Studies):<o:p></o:p></h1>

<ol start="1" style="margin-top: 0in;" type="1">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l7 level1 lfo13; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;"><b>Muraoka’s Greek-English Lexicon of the Septuagint
     (GELS)</b> –<o:p></o:p></li>
 <ul style="margin-top: 0in;" type="circle">
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l7 level2 lfo13; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;">Focuses on the Septuagint (LXX), the Greek
      translation of the Hebrew Bible, and is a valuable resource for scholars
      studying how Greek was used in the translation of Hebrew scriptures.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l7 level2 lfo13; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Pros:</b> Focused on the Septuagint, valuable
      for understanding the translation process and choices.<o:p></o:p></li>
  <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l7 level2 lfo13; tab-stops: list 1.0in; text-align: left;"><b>Cons:</b> Limited to the LXX; more specialized
      than BDAG or LSJ.<o:p></o:p></li>
 </ul>
</ol>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal">These lexicons are widely
accepted in both academic and theological studies, even older ones like <b>BDB</b>
and <b>Thayer's</b>, as long as they're used with an understanding of their
limitations and supplemented by more modern scholarship.<o:p></o:p></p>

<h1>Strong's is generally discouraged.<o:p></o:p></h1>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><b>Note: Limitations of
Using Strong's Concordance in Modern Academic Biblical Studies<o:p></o:p></b></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal">Strong's Concordance, a
popular reference tool for biblical languages, is not commonly used in modern
academic biblical studies due to several significant limitations:<o:p></o:p></p>

<ol start="1" style="margin-top: 0in;" type="1">
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l10 level1 lfo14; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;">Contextual Nuances: Strong's lists all possible
     definitions for Hebrew and Greek words without considering their specific
     contexts. Words can have varied meanings depending on their usage, similar
     to how the phrase "that's hot" in English can mean physically
     warm, sexually appealing, popular, or even contentious. Academic
     scholarship requires understanding words within their specific textual and
     historical contexts to accurately interpret their meanings.<o:p></o:p></li>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l10 level1 lfo14; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;">Outdated and Over-simplified: The definitions in
     Strong's are based on 19th-century scholarship and tend to oversimplify
     complex linguistic phenomena. Modern biblical studies use advanced
     lexicons like HALOT or BDAG, which provide updated and nuanced language
     analysis.<o:p></o:p></li>
 <li class="MsoNormal" style="mso-list: l10 level1 lfo14; tab-stops: list .5in; text-align: left;">Lacks Critical Engagement: Strong's Concordance does
     not address textual variants, historical linguistics, or the evolution of
     language, all of which are crucial for in-depth academic research.<o:p></o:p></li>
</ol>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal">Due to these issues,
scholars opt for more comprehensive and critically-engaged resources that
consider the broader linguistic and cultural settings of biblical texts.<o:p></o:p></p>

<p align="left" class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p><br /><br />

<span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;;">Shalom&nbsp;<span face="sans-serif" style="background-color: white; color: #202122; font-size: 14px;">שָׁלוֹם</span>: Live Long and Prosper!</span>
<br />

<div class="MsoNormal"><span style="font-family: &quot;tempus sans itc&quot;; font-size: 18pt;">Darrell Wolfe</span>
</div>

Storyteller | Writer | Thinker | Consultant | Freelancer | Bible Nerd
<i>*Written withs some editing and research assistance from ChatGPT-4o</i>
<br /><br /><br />

</div>
